data.title: Securities Fraud Investigation Into V.F. Corporation (VFC) Announced – Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a Leading Securities Fraud Law Firm
data.description:
Securities Fraud Investigation Into V.F. Corporation (VFC) Announced – Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a Leading Securities Fraud Law Firm

Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a leading national shareholder rights law firm, today announced that it has commenced an investigation on behalf of V.F. Corporation (“VFC” or the “Company”) (NYSE: VFC) investors concerning the Company’s possible violations of the federal securities laws.

IF YOU ARE AN INVESTOR WHO LOST MONEY ON V.F. CORPORATION (VFC), CLICK HERE TO INQUIRE ABOUT POTENTIALLY PURSUING CLAIMS TO RECOVER YOUR LOSS.

What Happened?

On May 21, 2025, VFC released its fourth quarter and full year fiscal 2025 financial results, revealing a significant decline in the growth trajectory of the Vans brand, climbing to a 20% loss in the fourth quarter, compared to an 8% loss in the previous quarter. The Company noted that the decline would continue through the next quarter and stated that the poor results were “a direct effect of deliberately reduced revenue to eliminate unprofitable or unproductive businesses” and “an additional set of deliberate actions” already in-place but previously unannounced.

On this news, VFC’s stock price fell $2.28, or 15.8%, to close at $12.15 per share on May 21, 2025, thereby injuring investors.

Contact Us To Participate or Learn More:

If you wish to learn more about this action, or if you have any questions concerning this announcement or your rights or interests with respect to these matters, please contact us.

Charles Linehan, Esq.,
Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP,
1925 Century Park East, Suite 2100,
Los Angeles California 90067
Email: shareholders@glancylaw.com
Telephone: 310-201-9150 (Toll-Free: 888-773-9224)
Visit our website at www.glancylaw.com.
Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, Twitter, or Facebook.

Whistleblower Notice

Persons with non-public information regarding VFC should consider their options to aid the investigation or take advantage of the SEC Whistleblower Program. Under the program, whistleblowers who provide original information may receive rewards totaling up to 30 percent of any successful recovery made by the SEC. For more information, call Charles H. Linehan at 310-201-9150 or 888-773-9224 or email shareholders@glancylaw.com.

About Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP

Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP (“GPM”) is a premier law firm representing investors and consumers in securities litigation and other complex class action litigation. GPM has been consistently ranked in the Top 50 Securities Class Action Settlements by ISS Securities Class Action Services. In 2018, GPM was ranked a top five law firm in number of securities class action settlements, and a top six law firm for total dollar size of settlements.

With four offices across the country, GPM’s nearly 40 attorneys have won groundbreaking rulings and recovered billions of dollars for investors and consumers in securities, antitrust, consumer, and employment class actions. GPM’s lawyers have handled cases covering a wide spectrum of corporate misconduct and relating to nearly all industries and sectors. GPM’s past successes have been widely covered by leading news and industry publications such as The Wall Street Journal, The Financial Times, Bloomberg Businessweek, Reuters, the Associated Press, Barron’s, Investor’s Business Daily, Forbes, and Money.

This press release may be considered Attorney Advertising in some jurisdictions under the applicable law and ethical rules.

data.title: Deadline Soon: RxSight, Inc. (RXST) Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz About Securities Fraud Lawsuit
data.description:
Deadline Soon: RxSight, Inc. (RXST) Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz About Securities Fraud Lawsuit

The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz reminds investors of the upcoming September 22, 2025 deadline to participate as a lead plaintiff in the securities fraud class action lawsuit filed on behalf of investors who acquired RxSight, Inc. (“RxSight” or the “Company”) (NASDAQ: RXST) securities between May 7, 2024 and July 8, 2025, inclusive (the “Class Period”).

IF YOU ARE AN INVESTOR WHO LOST MONEY ON RXSIGHT, INC. (RXST), CLICK HERE TO PARTICIPATE IN THE SECURITIES FRAUD LAWSUIT.

What Happened?

On July 8, 2025, after the market closed, RxSight reported preliminary second quarter 2025 financial results, revealing significant declines in LDD sales, LAL utilization, and overall revenue. The Company also lowered its full year 2025 guidance by approximately $42.5 million at the midpoint. The Company’s Chief Executive Officer, Ronald Kurtz, disclosed that “[a]doption challenges over the last few quarters have been a primary reason for the LDD stall.”

On this news, RxSight’s stock price fell $4.84, or 37.8%, to close at $7.95 per share on July 9, 2025, on unusually heavy trading volume.

What Is The Lawsuit About?

The complaint filed in this class action alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, Defendants failed to disclose to investors that: (1) the Company was experiencing “adoption challenges” and/or structural issues resulting in declines in sales and utilization; (2) Defendants had overstated the demand for RxSight’s products; (3) as a result, RxSight was unlikely to meet its own previously issued financial guidance for fiscal year 2025; and (4) that, as a result of the foregoing, Defendants’ positive statements about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.

If you purchased or otherwise acquired RxSight securities between May 7, 2024 and July 8, 2025, the deadline to seek appointment as the lead plaintiff in the securities fraud class action is September 22, 2025.

Contact Us To Participate or Learn More:

If you wish to learn more about this class action, or if you have any questions concerning this announcement or your rights or interests with respect to the pending class action lawsuit, please contact us:

Frank R. Cruz
The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz,

data.title: Social Security commissioner says younger generations may have a different set of rules. Should Gen Z even plan for it?
data.description:
By Alessandra Malito

The program's trust funds are expected to be depleted in less than a decade

Some young Americans are trying not to rely on Social Security income in their future retirement plans.

Christopher Hendrix sees Social Security as a "supplement" to his retirement income in the future, but not as something he should expect or rely on.

Hendrix, 24, and a National Youth Advisory Board member for the nonprofit advocacy organization Young Invincibles, said his retirement plan involves saving as much as he can. He recently started working with a financial adviser and has learned about the numerous types of retirement-income sources, such as employer-sponsored 401(k) plans, Roth IRAs, pensions and life insurance.

"I don't want to depend on something that's not guaranteed for me and my generation," he said. "I think there should be other avenues."

Hendrix is not the only young person to question his chances of getting Social Security one day, and perhaps with good reason. The program is currently facing insolvency within the next decade, and has recently been in the crosshairs as legislators and unions fight federal government officials and the Department of Government Efficiency over privacy concerns involving its sensitive data.

Millennials and Generation Z, more than any others, are most at risk of seeing a different Social Security than is known today - and perhaps a change to benefits - because of the adjustments lawmakers may need to make to keep the program afloat. Some of those adjustments include raising the full retirement age, which is 67 for anyone born in and after 1960, or adjusting the payroll-tax rate.

Even Social Security Administration Commissioner Frank Bisignano said in a recent interview with Maria Bartiromo on FOX Business that younger generations may see a different program.

"Remember, most people told you and me Social Security wasn't going to be around," Bisignano told Bartiromo. "And so the generations that are coming in will probably have a different set of rules than we had."

As a result, many younger workers are reconsidering how they'll incorporate Social Security into their future financial plans, if at all, financial advisers say. When planning for the future, some assume they'll get a lower-than-expected benefit. Some choose not to include Social Security in their plans at all.

But it may be short-sighted for young people to write Social Security off completely, experts say. How much Social Security actually accounts for a person's retirement income depends on how much they've saved outside of the program, as well as any other income streams such as a pension.

"I definitely hear that a lot from younger generations," said Martha Shedden, president and co-founder of the National Association of Registered Social Security Analysts. "They flatly state, 'It's not going to be there for me.'"

But that's simply not true, Shedden said. "Social Security is not going bankrupt. The whole program is not going to stop. The worst that will happen, if nothing is done, is benefits will be reduced."

Not the first time - maybe not the last

When Tim Knotts was in college more than 40 years ago, he didn't think Social Security would be around when he got to retirement.

"Social Security was seriously in trouble," Knotts said.

Knotts, a certified financial planner at the Hogan-Knotts Financial Group, held on to that belief until his 40s, when he got serious about his own retirement planning. Prior to that, he was too focused on graduating college, building a career and raising a family, he said. He watched in the early 1980s as lawmakers made major changes to the program to keep it afloat, including raising the full retirement age for beneficiaries. He also saw how slow Congress was to act when Social Security's finances were falling short.

Now, as he prepares to turn 65, Knotts is watching a similar drama play out. Young Americans then, as now, wonder if they'll get any return from the years they've paid into the system. Overall, confidence in the program has dwindled in recent years, from 43% in 2020 to 36% in 2025, according to an AARP report. Younger adults in particular have shown even more apprehension, with just a quarter of people between the ages of 18 and 49 saying they are confident in the program's future.

Given the turmoil surrounding the program, it's not surprising that some are hesitant to rely on Social Security as they plan their futures. Even still, ignoring it as part of your retirement strategy could be a mistake, said Knotts.

"If you do your retirement plan and ignore Social Security, that's a super conservative way to do that," Knotts said. "The problem is, for some people, Social Security can become a significant portion of their retirement income, and you have to save a heck of a lot more money to make up that difference."

Knotts gave an example: If someone assumes they'll live until 92, there is a 25-year time frame between age 67, which is the full retirement age for anyone born in 1960 and after, and age 92. If that person expects to receive $3,000 a month from Social Security, or $36,000 a year, assuming a 3% inflation rate, they'd have to save more than $600,000 to cover that lack of income from Social Security, Knotts noted. Depending on when that person begins saving for the difference, their savings rate would likely need to be quite aggressive to cover the gap.

When clients - even those in their 60s - ask Knotts about Social Security's future and where it fits in their retirement plans, he tells them what he remembers of the political discussions in 1982.

"I think it is a legitimate position if you're a younger person to believe that Social Security will not be around when you get to full retirement age," he said. "I held that position for many years."

Advising young clients

Concerns about the program's future are fueling changes in retirement strategy from members of younger generations as well as those who advise them. Easton Price, a certified financial planner at Prosperity Wealth Planning, doesn't work primarily with millennials, but he himself does fall somewhere between millennial and Gen Z. He said when he talks to people in his age group, he often suggests omitting Social Security from their plans.

"If Social Security is not there, they will not have planned for it, and if it is there, it's icing on the cake," he said. "I don't like ever planning for things I don't actually have control over. Hope for the best, but plan for the worst."

Other financial planners don't agree with that strategy - and say that by not incorporating Social Security income at all in their retirement plans, their clients may be socking too much money away and sacrificing funds they could use elsewhere in the present.

Kevin Brady, a certified financial planner and senior vice president at Wealthspire Advisors, said he prefers to include Social Security like it is expected in his clients' plans, unless they request to change it. He tells his clients the program can change - and while it would likely affect their generation if it did, he points out that it is an "ongoing conversation." There are also other components to their retirement plans, including their own savings, which they control.

"I highly doubt it won't be there in some form," Brady said. "Will there be meaningful changes to it? That's possible. Even with current assumptions, they'd get 70% to 80% if there were no changes. That's not great, but it is a far cry from nothing."

Many advisers use software when working on retirement plans to provide clients with various outlooks that take into consideration different types of investment and saving strategies, inflation and return assumptions, and other possible scenarios.

For Catherine Valega, a certified financial planner at Green Bee Advisory, showing these models and the effects of Social Security to her younger clients opens up an educational conversation about the program, what it means and how it can fit into their overall financial plans.

"I'm having more conversations about why this is an important part of your financial plan," she said. "They don't know what it is; no one has ever explained it. I like to explain why they need to take it seriously and what this potentially turns into when they decide to claim it."

When Valega does discuss the impact Social Security has on retirement planning, she also discusses how the ways in which people get paid - such as off the books - can affect future income, and the various claiming strategies available at retirement.

The challenges facing Social Security, and what young generations need to do

Younger generations often hesitate to incorporate Social Security into their plans because of its uncertainty. "There's a weird dichotomy where people say, 'I know I need to rely on it, but I don't know if I can rely on it," said Alexander Lundrigan, policy and advocacy manager at Young Invincibles who focuses on higher education and the workforce.

Almost since the Social Security program was established, it's become a rite of passage of sorts for future generations to question whether it will be available to them. Still, while Congress has never let Social Security falter, lawmakers may wait until the last moment to make any meaningful changes, if history is any indication.

In the early 1980s, Social Security's trust funds were nearly depleted before Congress took action and made major changes that were signed into law in 1983. The program was in such disarray that it had to borrow funds to stay afloat, former Social Security Administration Commissioner John Svahn recalled to MarketWatch in an interview earlier this year.

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

09-19-25 1330ET

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Microsoft's stock has been in a rut since earnings. What's going on?
data.description:
By Emily Bary

Analysts offer a few theories for the seemingly 'unexplainable' way the tech giant's stock has fallen out of favor in recent weeks

Microsoft has fallen out of favor with Big Tech investors in recent weeks.

Microsoft Corp. had arguably the strongest earnings report within Big Tech last quarter, at least relative to investor expectations. Yet its stock is flat since just before the results came out, as nearly every other member of the "Magnificent Seven" has moved higher.

Executives at Microsoft (MSFT) "sound fantastic in buy-side meetings as of late," according to Mizuho desk-based analyst Jordan Klein, and the company is levered to OpenAI's booming demand as well as upbeat cloud trends even outside of artificial intelligence. Microsoft has also told investors what to expect on margins for the next 12 months through guidance that was "good" and added clarity, Klein noted.

So why don't investors seem very interested in the stock?

It's a dynamic that's "unexplainable," Klein wrote on Friday, noting he's gotten "zero good answers" when he's posed the question lately in nearly all of his investor conversations.

But whether there are no answers or just no good answers may be more of the issue. Klein does have a few theories around why investors may be dodging Microsoft's stock lately. For one, they may be worried about how Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing growth will fare once OpenAI "jumps ship and takes their act on road solo." OpenAI reportedly has a $300 billion cloud deal with Oracle Corp. (ORCL) over the coming years that "implies they want off ... Azure sooner vs. later," according to Klein.

Read: Why the $300 billion Oracle-OpenAI deal could be fueling an AI bubble

There's also the fact that Microsoft is a software company, and Klein has seen some investors rotate out of the software sector in favor of semiconductors.

Evercore ISI analyst Kirk Materne explored the phenomenon as well in a note to clients last week. At the time of his Sept. 9 report, he said he was starting to get more investor questions about Microsoft's lagging postearnings stock performance relative to the S&P 500 SPX.

"On a fundamental basis, we don't see much to justify the recent underperformance," he wrote. "That said, the new 'marketecture' around the 'Mag 7' means that [Microsoft] will, at times, lose some momentum if another 'Mag 7' member is seeing a material shift in sentiment" - which had been happening as Apple Inc.'s stock (AAPL) began to see new shine in the eyes of investors.

Materne noted there was a high bar for Microsoft's Azure cloud-computing unit after the company stunned Wall Street with 39% growth last quarter, "but the trends underpinning that growth - namely ChatGPT inferencing and solid enterprise demand - seem stable."

Don't miss: Why Microsoft's 'must-own' stock looks even more like a no-brainer after earnings

-Emily Bary

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-19-25 1335ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Trump has a chance to do what he claims to be so good at: make a deal with China on energy
data.description:
By Paul Brandus

How a natural-gas deal could boost energy stocks and U.S.-China relations

Chinese President Xi Jinping and U.S. President Donald Trump have something the other wants: natural gas. China needs it; the U.S. has it.

The recent meeting in China among leaders of an "Axis of Opposition" - China, Russia, India and other countries that resent American power and the U.S.-led world order - was heavy on geopolitical symbolism. But for investors, the real story, underneath the photo ops and champagne toasts, concerns energy.

With Russian President Vladimir Putin apparently intending to continue the war he started in Ukraine, he's scrambling to finance it. Oil and gas are what he has to sell, and, fortunately for him, India and China - the world's two most populous countries - are energy-hungry buyers. This year, India and China have each been buying about 2 million barrels of crude a day from Russia, according to data from the International Energy Agency. That's 4 million barrels of discounted oil that China and India don't need to buy on the open market.

China and India know they have Putin in a corner, and they're squeezing Moscow. Indian refiners reportedly want even steeper discounts to compensate for the risks - compliance, reputational and banking - they take in dealing with Russia .

President Donald Trump, angling perhaps for sales of crude from the U.S. - the world's largest producer of oil - doesn't want any Russian oil sales to India. An original 25% tariff on Indian exports to the U.S. was doubled by Trump after New Delhi refused to halt its oil imports.

The problem with Trump's demand is that it's bumping up against a prior American policy, coordinated with other G-7 nations, to allow Russian oil exports to India, China and other buyers such as Turkey, through a price-cap scheme. The idea was to squeeze Putin's war machine by forcing him to sell at lower prices.

Why not really put the screws to the Kremlin by hitting Russia with a total embargo? Because allowing Putin to sell oil maintains an overall equilibrium in global markets while simultaneously preventing energy-price shocks. Such an embargo could drive prices up globally - and raise gasoline prices in the U.S. That's probably the last thing wanted by Trump, who is anxious to keep inflation in check. So far, gasoline prices have risen slightly since he took office, inching up about 6 cents to a nationwide average of $3.26 a gallon, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (a federal agency).

But what's interesting here -and perhaps could actually support Trump's idea of a deeper oil embargo against Russia - is that oil prices have fallen sharply since Putin went into Ukraine more than 31/2 years ago. Brent crude (BRN00), for example, traded at about $98 a barrel in February 2022; it's now in the upper $60s. West Texas Intermediate (WBS00)? Similar story: $91 to about $63.

A tighter embargo on Putin now would inflict more pain on the struggling Russian economy - perhaps weakening his position vis-a-vis Ukraine - and would also raise global prices. But since Brent and WTI are priced around a third lower than when the Ukraine war started, Western consumers could endure a modest price hike. Just an idea. This helps explain why Putin is eager, perhaps desperately so, to sell even more oil to China.

Read: Russia and China's gas alliance is a bluff. Will Washington call it?

Energy investors should also keep their eye on Russia's ongoing scramble to find new customers for its natural gas. The European Union says it obtained about 45% of its gas from Putin before the war started; late last year that was down to 19%, and the E.U.'s goal is to phase out Russian natural gas completely by 2027. What was once Putin's biggest customer will soon be gone.

What will Putin do? Running out of customers, Putin is pivoting to China. He's desperate for the so-called Power of Siberia 2 pipeline to be built. It would carry natural gas some 1,600 miles through Siberia, through Mongolia, and into northern China. But like India, which is using Moscow's economic weakness as leverage, China's President Xi Jinping also knows how to squeeze the Russians. He has dragged his heels on the project for years.

The pipeline "could reshape global energy" and supply more than a fifth of China's projected gas needs in 2030, according to a recent analysis by the Washington-based Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS). That's if it gets built. Chinese and Russian officials have signed what's described as a "legally binding memorandum" - though the CSIS analysis said that "Reportedly, no agreement has been reached on price, timeline, or builder." It could be years before Russian gas flows to China and money comes to Moscow. That's too bad for Putin. He's playing for time; but that doesn't seem to be Xi's problem.

Once again, there's an American angle. Trump has made no secret of his desire to boost exports of liquefied natural gas (LNG) to the world. Qatar and Australia are now the biggest suppliers of LNG to China, and Trump would love a piece of that action. What might this mean for broader U.S.-Chinese issues, such as the ongoing tariff spat between Washington and Beijing? The world's two economic giants have extended their pause on higher tariffs through early November. Both Trump and Xi have something the other wants. China needs gas, the U.S. has it. Here's an opportunity for Trump to do what he claims to be good at: making a deal.

More: Foreign investors are still buying U.S. assets in 2025 - but there is one catch

Also read: China and Russia vie to dominate in energy. Trump Is handing them the win.

-Paul Brandus

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-19-25 1328ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Deadline Soon: Fiserv, Inc. (FI) Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz About Securities Fraud Lawsuit
data.description:
Deadline Soon: Fiserv, Inc. (FI) Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz About Securities Fraud Lawsuit

The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz reminds investors of the upcoming DATEdeadline to participate as a lead plaintiff in the securities fraud class action lawsuit filed on behalf of investors who acquired Fiserv, Inc. (“Fiserv” or the “Company”) (NYSE: FI) common stock between July 24, 2024 and July 22, 2025, inclusive (the “Class Period”).

IF YOU ARE AN INVESTOR WHO LOST MONEY ON FISERV, INC. (FI), CLICK HERE TO PARTICIPATE IN THE SECURITIES FRAUD LAWSUIT.

What Happened?

On April 24, 2025, Fiserv reported that the gross payment volume (“GPV”) of its merchant payment gateway, Clover, had only seen a growth of 8% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 14% to 17% from the previous year. The Company attributed the slowing growth to lower 2025 transaction volumes from merchants using the older payment gateway, Payeezy, who had converted to Clover.

On this news, Fiserv’s stock price fell $40.20, or 18.5%, to close at $176.90 per share on April 24, 2025, thereby injuring investors.

Then, on May 15, 2025, Fiserv disclosed that GPV growth deceleration would continue throughout 2025. On this news, Fiserv’s stock price fell $30.73, or 16.2%, to close at $159.13 per share on May 15, 2025.

Then, on July 23, 2025, Fiserv lowered the top end of its full-year organic growth guidance range and confirmed that its quarterly organic revenue in the Merchant segment had decelerated to 9% year-over-year from 11% in the previous quarter.

On this news, Fiserv’s stock price fell $22.98, or 13.8%, to close at $143.00 per share on July 23, 2025, thereby injuring investors further.

What Is The Lawsuit About?

The complaint filed in this class action alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, Defendants failed to disclose to investors that: (1) due to cost issues and other problems with its Payeezy platform, Fiserv forced Payeezy merchants to migrate to its Clover platform; (2) Clover’s revenue growth and GPV growth were temporarily and unsustainably boosted by these forced conversions, which concealed a slowdown in new merchant business; (3) shortly after these conversions, a significant portion of former Payeezy merchants switched to competing solutions due to Clover’s high pricing, inadequate customer service, and other issues; (4) as a result of these merchant losses, Clover’s GPV growth was significantly slowing, and its revenue growth was unsustainable; and (5) as a result, Defendants’ positive statements about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis at all relevant times.

If you purchased or otherwise acquired Fiserv securities between July 24, 2024 and July 22, 2025, the deadline to seek appointment as the lead plaintiff in the securities fraud class action is September 22, 2025.

Contact Us To Participate or Learn More:

If you wish to learn more about this class action, or if you have any questions concerning this announcement or your rights or interests with respect to the pending class action lawsuit, please contact us:
Frank R. Cruz
The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz,
2121 Avenue of the Stars, Suite 800,
Century City, California 90067
Email us at: info@frankcruzlaw.com
Call us at: 310-914-5007

Follow us for updates on Twitter: twitter.com/FRC_LAW

If you inquire by email, please include your mailing address, telephone number, and number of shares purchased.

To be a member of the class action you need not take any action at this time; you may retain counsel of your choice or take no action and remain an absent member of the class action. This press release may be considered Attorney Advertising in some jurisdictions under the applicable law and ethical rules.

The Law Offices of Frank R. Cruz, Los Angeles

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: Stephen Miran Makes His Case For More-and Bigger-Interest Rate Cuts | Stocks ...
data.description:
Stephen Miran Makes His Case For More-and Bigger-Interest Rate Cuts

The newest Fed governor breaks ranks with board members, saying he doesn't see inflation from tariffs.

----

Stocks Rise as Markets Focus on U.S.-China Ties

U.S. stocks rose after President Trump announced a TikTok deal with Chinese leader Xi Jinping after the two spoke today.

----

Trump's Team Explores Government-Backed Manufacturing Boost

Discussions center on how to use money from a $550 billion fund to spur the construction of factories and other infrastructure in the U.S.

----

Under Trump Pressure, EU Proposes Going After Chinese Companies Buying Russia Oil

The bloc unveiled measures that would seek to squeeze Russia, but wouldn't fully meet demands by President Trump.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Data in Focus as Investors Gauge Pace of Future Rate Cuts

U.S. economic data, including PCE inflation, purchasing managers' surveys and durable goods figures, will be closely monitored in the week ahead.

----

Greece Seeks Foreign Investors to Buy Into Recovery Story

EXCLUSIVE: The government's pitch to investors follows an acceleration of economic growth in the second quarter, having already outpaced the European average in 2024.

----

House Passes GOP Stopgap Spending Bill Ahead of Shutdown

Democrats have demanded Republicans include $1 trillion in healthcare funds to support a stopgap funding measure.

----

U.K. Retail Sales Climbed Again in August

Retail volumes were 0.5% higher on month in August, the same increase as July, with improvements seen amongst clothing stores, butchers, bakers and non-store retailing.

----

Bank of Japan to Start Offloading More Assets

Billions in sales are planned in a program that could take decades.

----

U.K. Consumer Sentiment Falls Back

The mood of British consumers dampened a little this month, with expectations of a slowing economy and the potential for tax increases later this year threatening an even greater downturn.

----

Japan Consumer Prices Rise, But at Slower Pace

Japan's consumer prices rose at a slower pace in August but remained well above the central bank's 2% target, backing the case for an interest-rate increase.

----

Why the Fed Rate Cut Won't Ease the Government's Debt Problem

The move will likely reduce U.S. borrowing costs for short-term Treasury bills, but annual interest expense won't shrink much.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 1 PM ET: Trump Says U.S. and China Approve TikTok Deal After Call With Xi | Nvidia, ...

Trump Says U.S. and China Approve TikTok Deal After Call With Xi

data.title: Securities Fraud Investigation Into Stride, Inc. (LRN) Announced – Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a Leading Securities Fraud Law Firm
data.description:
Securities Fraud Investigation Into Stride, Inc. (LRN) Announced – Investors Who Lost Money Urged To Contact Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a Leading Securities Fraud Law Firm

Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP, a leading national shareholder rights law firm, today announced that it has commenced an investigation on behalf of Stride, Inc. (“Stride” or the “Company”) (NYSE: LRN) investors concerning the Company’s possible violations of the federal securities laws.

IF YOU ARE AN INVESTOR WHO LOST MONEY ON STRIDE, INC. (LRN), CLICK HERE TO INQUIRE ABOUT POTENTIALLY PURSUING CLAIMS TO RECOVER YOUR LOSS.

What Happened?

On September 14, 2025, Simply Wall St. published a report stating that the Gallup-McKinley County Schools Board of Education had filed a complaint against Stride, alleging fraud, deceptive trade practices, systemic violations of law, and intentional and tortious misconduct, including inflating enrollment numbers by retaining "ghost students" on rolls to secure state funding per student and ignoring compliance requirements, including background checks and licensure laws for its employees.

On this news, Stride’s stock price fell $18.60, or 11.7%, to close at $139.76 per share on September 15, 2025, thereby injuring investors.

Contact Us To Participate or Learn More:

If you wish to learn more about this action, or if you have any questions concerning this announcement or your rights or interests with respect to these matters, please contact us.

Charles Linehan, Esq.
Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP
1925 Century Park East, Suite 2100
Los Angeles, California 90067
Email: shareholders@glancylaw.com
Telephone: 310-201-9150 (Toll-Free: 888-773-9224)
Visit our website at www.glancylaw.com.
Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, Twitter, or Facebook.

Whistleblower Notice

Persons with non-public information regarding Stride should consider their options to aid the investigation or take advantage of the SEC Whistleblower Program. Under the program, whistleblowers who provide original information may receive rewards totaling up to 30 percent of any successful recovery made by the SEC. For more information, call Charles H. Linehan at 310-201-9150 or 888-773-9224 or email shareholders@glancylaw.com.

About Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP

Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP (“GPM”) is a premier law firm representing investors and consumers in securities litigation and other complex class action litigation. GPM has been consistently ranked in the Top 50 Securities Class Action Settlements by ISS Securities Class Action Services. In 2018, GPM was ranked a top five law firm in number of securities class action settlements, and a top six law firm for total dollar size of settlements.

With four offices across the country, GPM’s nearly 40 attorneys have won groundbreaking rulings and recovered billions of dollars for investors and consumers in securities, antitrust, consumer, and employment class actions. GPM’s lawyers have handled cases covering a wide spectrum of corporate misconduct and relating to nearly all industries and sectors. GPM’s past successes have been widely covered by leading news and industry publications such as The Wall Street Journal, The Financial Times, Bloomberg Businessweek, Reuters, the Associated Press, Barron’s, Investor’s Business Daily, Forbes, and Money.

This press release may be considered Attorney Advertising in some jurisdictions under the applicable law and ethical rules.

Glancy Prongay & Murray LLP
1925 Century Park East, Suite 2100
Los Angeles, CA 90067
Charles Linehan
Email: shareholders@glancylaw.com
Telephone: 310-201-9150
Toll-Free: 888-773-9224
Visit our website at: www.glancylaw.com.

data.title: I'm 88 and leaving my $450,000 estate to my son because he has children. Is that wrong?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'My other children have relationships, but they are unmarried and have no children'

"My other children have relationships, but they are unmarried and have no children." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I'm 88 and co-own a home with my youngest son, who lives in another state with his wife. He pays the mortgage and taxes, and I pay for all other upkeep and utilities. I have Vanguard stocks valued at about $350,000 and two savings accounts each of which has $100,000. He is my major beneficiary because he is my only married son and father to my two grandsons.

My other children have relationships, but they are unmarried and have no children. My daughter is the named beneficiary of my small IRA, valued at around $40,000. My middle son is well-to-do and is in a long-term domestic partnership. He is the named beneficiary of a small stock valued at about $50,000.

My third and eldest son is single, owns his own home and is financially secure. He is not the beneficiary of ANY of my assets because I gifted him $10,000 five years ago and he was the major recipient of his mother's estate, worth about $400,000, leaving only a small amount to my youngest son, and excluding my middle son.

Is my estate plan fair? I have no will. Do I need one?

Father of Four

Related: 'I have zero regrets': I'm 84 and estranged from my two adult sons. My 48-year-old wife will get my seven-figure estate. Is that selfish?

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Every family is unique. Yours is no different.

Dear Father,

Your estate plan is fair if you believe it to be fair.

You earned it, so on this occasion, I'm not going to tell you what to do with it. I understand that you believe your youngest son will pass this money down to his children - meaning they will get the benefit of it - and you have a relationship with them too, so you are accounting for them collectively and individually (when that time comes and your son passes away).

Your eldest son received $400,000 from his mother, so he's good to go. Your two middle children will receive a token amount, which I am sure they will appreciate even if they have a moment or two of wondering why they received less. If you were to split your $550,000 four ways, excluding your eldest son from the pot, your kids would each receive just over $180,000.

Leaving your estate to one son makes a bigger impact on his life; he's also paying your mortgage and, from that fact alone, I assume you have a closer relationship with him. All things being equal - the closeness of your relationships, financial statuses etc. - you would split your estate equally. While ideal for the heirs, that's not how life works.

Do I personally agree with you leaving more money to one son because he has children, and your other children don't? Not really, but it's hard to judge from afar. You are giving him preferential treatment, and he is doing the same by co-owning a home with you and paying your mortgage and property taxes. So it's quid-pro-quo.

Leaving your estate to one son makes a bigger impact on his life; he's also paying your mortgage and, from that fact alone, I assume you have a closer relationship with him.

You belong to a large cohort. You are one of the approximately 33% of Americans who don't have a will, according to a 2024 Caring.com survey. You can do quite a lot to bypass a will, including listing your preferred heir as a beneficiary on your savings accounts, so they go to him upon your passing, thereby avoiding probate. Similarly, your share of your home.

What kind of ownership agreement do you have with your youngest son? If you have joint tenancy with the right of survivorship (JTWOS) that gives all owners an equal share of the property and does not allow one owner to add another person to the deed and, importantly, if one owner dies, their share of the property goes to the other owner.

What will happen to any vehicles in your name? Would you like to bequeath some mementos to friends, neighbors or extended family members? If you are leaving your other children sums of money, you may wish to do that via a will, stating that your youngest son will receive everything else. (It's always good to name him, in case you have a younger son you were not aware of!)

Some medical considerations: An advance healthcare directive informs your doctors what action you want them to take if you are unable to make those decisions yourself. You may wish to list your son as your healthcare proxy. Do you wish to be resuscitated if your breathing or heart stops? Also, ensure you have a successor on your POA and healthcare directive.

Wills also allow people to make statements of thanks, gratitude or appreciation, and bestow symbolic gestures to their children. Did your daughter always want to visit the Great Wall of China? Leave her a sum of money for that trip of a lifetime. Did your eldest son want to go to college to study ancient Welsh or English? Leave a stipend for him to do just that.

Every family is unique. Yours is no different.

Related: My daughter no longer speaks to me or my husband, and mocked our family values. Do we cut her out of her $2 million inheritance?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com.

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, tell me what you want to know more about, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

'Is this ethical?' I want to leave my home to my children from my first marriage - and not to my second husband.

I want to leave my home to my children from my first marriage - and not to my second husband. Is that wrong?

'My retirement is going to be a disaster': I'm 59 and have $45,000 in my 401(k). I earn $72,000. Am I doomed?

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-19-25 1307ET
